content.text: Structured output from language models is never guaranteed. Even with explicit JSON instructions and schema prompts, a model may produce truncated JSON, add prose before or after the object, swap field names, or omit required keys.

is the technique of catching those malformed outputs, formatting the exact validation error into a repair prompt, and re-invoking the model — giving it targeted feedback so it can self-correct rather than failing hard. Done well, this loop makes structured LLM output robust enough for production pipelines.

The parsing-and-retry loop sits between the raw LLM response and the downstream code that consumes structured data. After every generation, the loop attempts to deserialize the text into the expected format (JSON, YAML, a Pydantic model) and validate it against a schema. If that step succeeds, the parsed object is returned. If it fails — for any reason — the loop builds a short
that tells the model precisely what was wrong and asks it to output a corrected version, then calls the LLM again.
The key conceptual move is feeding
validation errors back as model input

. Rather than ignoring the LLM's attempt or silently discarding it and regenerating from scratch, the system keeps the conversation context, appends the error message, and asks for a targeted fix. This exploits the model's ability to understand its own mistakes when they are described clearly.

A hard retry cap — typically two to three attempts — prevents infinite loops when the model is fundamentally unable to produce the required structure (e.g., the schema is ambiguous or the context window is exhausted). When all retries are spent, the loop raises a structured exception or invokes a fallback strategy.

Agentic systems depend on structured outputs far more than conversational chatbots do. A code-writing agent that emits malformed tool-call JSON stalls the entire pipeline. A data-extraction agent that returns a missing field breaks downstream analytics. The cost of a single hard failure — user-visible error, orphaned task, wasted prior computation — is high, whereas the cost of one extra LLM call is small.
Empirically, well-crafted prompts with schema examples still fail to produce valid output in roughly

depending on model size, schema complexity, and temperature. In a pipeline that makes dozens of structured calls, even a 5 % per-call failure rate compounds quickly: a ten-step agentic workflow would fail on at least one step more than 40 % of the time if failures are not caught and corrected. Parsing retries bring per-step failure rates down to under 1 %, making the overall pipeline reliability tractable.
Beyond reliability, retries also improve developer experience. Instead of debugging cryptic downstream

exceptions caused by unexpected model output shapes, developers get clear parse-failure logs pinpointing which field was missing, which type was wrong, and how many repair attempts were needed — actionable signals for prompt improvement.

content: A single parse-and-retry loop with two repair attempts reduces hard failures by an order of magnitude compared to no retry logic.

Including the exact validation error in the repair prompt is far more effective than blind regeneration; the model fixes the specific problem rather than producing a completely different (and potentially still-invalid) output.
Most failures are caught and corrected in the first repair attempt. The added latency is one extra LLM round-trip in the minority of calls that fail, not every call.
Every failed parse attempt is a logged event with the raw text, the error message, and the attempt number — forming a feedback loop for prompt engineering.
Formalizing the expected output as a Pydantic model or JSON Schema document forces the developer to be explicit about data contracts, which improves system design beyond just retry logic.
Parsing retries compose cleanly with semantic validators (range checks, business-rule assertions) and with human-in-the-loop escalation for unresolvable failures.

content.text: Added latency and cost on failures.

content: Each retry is a full LLM call. If failure rates are high (e.g., the schema is too complex or the prompt is miscalibrated), the retry loop multiplies both latency and API cost.

content.text: Not a substitute for good prompting.

content: Retries fix occasional failures; they do not fix a fundamentally wrong prompt. If the model consistently misunderstands the schema, the fix is prompt engineering, not more retries.
Repair prompts append the error to the existing conversation. In long pipelines, accumulated context can approach the model's window limit, making repair prompts expensive or impossible.
A schema-valid object is not necessarily semantically correct. Retries guarantee structural conformance, not that field values make sense for the task.
When all retries are exhausted, the system must decide whether to raise, fallback to a default, or escalate to a human. This fallback logic is easy to forget during development.

content.text: The loop has four phases: generation, parsing, validation, and repair. The first attempt begins with the normal task prompt. On success the loop exits immediately. On any failure, a repair cycle begins.
The caller invokes the LLM with a task prompt that includes the schema definition (inline JSON Schema or a typed Pydantic class rendered as a schema string) and an output format instruction such as
"Respond with valid JSON matching the schema. Output only the JSON object, no prose."
The model returns a raw text string.
The raw text is fed to a parser appropriate to the format:

content: in Python or
in JavaScript. Common failures include trailing commas, unquoted keys, and prose preamble like "Here is the JSON:".
the model often wraps JSON in fenced code blocks. A pre-processor strips the backtick fence before parsing.
(more tolerant of formatting than JSON, but ambiguous for booleans and integers).
construct the model directly from the parsed dict. Type coercion and field presence are checked simultaneously.

content.text: If parsing raises any exception, the raw error message (e.g.,
) is captured verbatim and passed to the repair phase.

Even when the text is syntactically valid JSON, the resulting object may not match the schema. A Pydantic model validates field types, required fields, enums, numeric ranges, and custom validators. The
it raises enumerates every violation as a structured list, which the repair formatter can serialize into a readable error string.
The repair prompt is constructed from three elements:

content: (or a compact reference to it, to avoid duplicating a long system prompt).
(the raw text that failed).
from the parser or validator, formatted clearly: "Validation failed: field 'priority' is required but missing; field 'score' must be an integer between 0 and 100, got 'high'."

content.text: The LLM processes this context and produces a new output. The attempt counter increments. If the new output passes parsing and validation, the loop returns it. If not, the cycle repeats until the counter reaches
The total number of LLM calls is bounded by:
gives at most three calls total — the initial attempt plus two repairs — which is sufficient for the vast majority of real failures while preventing runaway cost.

The diagram below shows the full data-flow through the parsing and retry loop. The happy path (top row) exits after a single generation. All failure paths converge at the Error Formatter, pass through the Retry Gate, and either loop back to the Parser with enriched context or exit to the Fallback handler.

attrs.alt: Parsing and Retry Loop Architecture — showing LLM output flowing through Parser, Schema Validator, Retry Gate, and Fallback

content.text: The Retry Gate is the critical component: it holds the attempt counter, enforces the ceiling, and decides whether to feed the enriched repair context back into the LLM or to trigger the fallback path. In practice this is a simple counter check wrapped around the generation call, but it must be explicit — implicit retry (e.g., via an HTTP library's built-in retry) bypasses the context enrichment that makes repair effective.
Parsing retries are appropriate whenever a pipeline consumes structured output from an LLM and a hard failure is more disruptive than one extra API call.

content: When an agent maps LLM output to function parameters, a missing or mistyped argument silently breaks behavior. Validate the argument object before calling the tool.
Extracting entities, dates, or key-value pairs from unstructured text into structured records for databases or spreadsheets.
Routing, tagging, or scoring tasks where the output must be one of a fixed set of values with additional fields.
Any pipeline where downstream steps depend on correctly shaped input from an upstream LLM call.
LLM-as-judge patterns where the judge must return a numeric score, a verdict enum, and a rationale string in a fixed schema.
Summaries, translations, and creative writing do not have a schema to validate against. Retrying makes no sense.
If the schema itself is so complex that the model cannot learn it from a prompt, retries will exhaust without success. Simplify the schema first.

If even a rare extra round-trip is unacceptable (e.g., real-time voice interfaces), prefer structured-output APIs (OpenAI structured outputs, Anthropic tool use) that guarantee format at the API level.
If failure rates exceed 30 %, retries are a band-aid. Fix the prompt or switch to a more capable model.

content.text: The minimal implementation is a loop that calls the LLM, tries to parse, catches exceptions, and retries with the error appended. The production implementation adds schema validation, structured error formatting, observability, and a fallback handler.
Use Pydantic v2 in Python or Zod in TypeScript to define the expected output shape. Include field descriptions and validators — these can be rendered into the prompt as a schema hint.
Step 2 — Build the prompt with schema hint
Render the JSON Schema representation of the Pydantic model into the system prompt so the model knows the exact expected shape.

import json
import logging
from typing import Type, TypeVar
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

T = TypeVar("T", bound=BaseModel)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def extract_json_block(text: str) -> str:
    """Strip markdown code fences if the model wrapped output in them."""
    text = text.strip()
    if text.startswith("```"):
        lines = text.split("\n")
        # drop first line (```json) and last line (```)
        text = "\n".join(lines[1:-1]).strip()
    return text

def parse_with_retry(
    llm_call,            # callable(messages) -> str
    messages: list,      # initial conversation messages
    model_class: Type[T],
    max_retries: int = 2,
) -> T:
    """
    Call llm_call with messages, parse/validate the response into model_class.
    On failure, append the error and retry up to max_retries times.
    Raises RuntimeError if all attempts fail.
    """
    attempt = 0
    current_messages = list(messages)
    last_error: str = ""

    while attempt <= max_retries:
        raw = llm_call(current_messages)
        logger.debug("Attempt %d raw output: %s", attempt, raw[:200])

        try:
            cleaned = extract_json_block(raw)
            data = json.loads(cleaned)
            result = model_class.model_validate(data)
            if attempt > 0:
                logger.info("Recovered after %d repair attempt(s)", attempt)
            return result

        except (json.JSONDecodeError, ValidationError, ValueError) as exc:
            last_error = str(exc)
            logger.warning("Attempt %d failed: %s", attempt, last_error)

            if attempt >= max_retries:
                break

            # Build repair prompt
            repair_msg = (
                f"Your previous response failed validation:\n\n"
                f"ERROR: {last_error}\n\n"
                f"Previous output:\n{raw}\n\n"
                "Return ONLY a corrected JSON object with no prose."
            )
            current_messages = current_messages + [
                {"role": "assistant", "content": raw},
                {"role": "user", "content": repair_msg},
            ]
            attempt += 1

    raise RuntimeError(
        f"Failed to parse valid output after {max_retries + 1} attempts. "
        f"Last error: {last_error}"
    )
to the Anthropic SDK and extracts a task assessment from a free-text description.

import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal

# Re-use parse_with_retry from the implementation section above.

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class TaskAssessment(BaseModel):
    task_id: str
    priority: Literal["low", "medium", "high", "critical"]
    effort_hours: float
    confidence: float

SYSTEM = (
    "Extract a TaskAssessment JSON from the user's description. "
    "Schema: {task_id: str, priority: low|medium|high|critical, "
    "effort_hours: float, confidence: 0.0-1.0}. "
    "Respond ONLY with the JSON object."
)

def llm_call(messages):
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=messages,
    )
    return response.content[0].text

user_text = (
    "We need to migrate the auth service to OAuth2. "
    "It's blocking our enterprise release — I'd say about 3 weeks of work."
)

assessment = parse_with_retry(
    llm_call=llm_call,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_text}],
    model_class=TaskAssessment,
    max_retries=2,
)

print(assessment.model_dump())
# {'task_id': 'auth-oauth2-migration', 'priority': 'critical',
#  'effort_hours': 120.0, 'confidence': 0.75}
Example 2 — JavaScript / TypeScript with Zod validation
The same pattern in JavaScript using the OpenAI SDK and Zod. The
method returns a result discriminated union rather than throwing, which integrates cleanly with the retry loop.

A contract-analysis pipeline at a legal-tech company processes hundreds of NDAs per day. Each document is chunked into 1,500-token sections, and an LLM extracts structured clause data — party names, effective date, term length, termination conditions, governing law — into a

Without retries, 8 % of extractions failed outright, mostly due to the model including prose commentary before the JSON object, or misformatting dates as strings instead of ISO-8601. Legal analysts had to manually review every failure. With a two-attempt retry loop that included the exact Pydantic error in the repair prompt, the failure rate dropped to 0.4 % — a 20× improvement. Only the residual 0.4 % required human review.
The repair prompt template used in production was deliberately minimal:

Including the schema hint again in the repair prompt was crucial: the model had sometimes drifted from the schema after long context, and repeating it in the repair message reset that drift. The pipeline logged every repair event with the section ID, attempt count, and error type, feeding a weekly prompt-quality review that progressively reduced the base failure rate.
Mistake 1 — Blind retry without error context

The most common mistake is retrying the exact same prompt without appending the validation error. This gives the model no information about what went wrong, so it simply generates another response with the same probability of failure.
Wrapping the entire retry loop in a bare
swallows failures silently, making debugging nearly impossible. Always log the error and re-raise or return a sentinel after max retries.
to a very high number or using a
loop risks runaway cost if the model is systematically unable to produce valid output for a given input. Always set an explicit ceiling and handle the exhaustion case.
Mistake 4 — Resending the full system prompt on every repair

If the system prompt is large (multi-page instructions, full schema documentation), resending it verbatim on each repair attempt wastes tokens and may approach the context limit. Keep the repair message focused: just the error, the previous output, and a compact schema reminder.
Mistake 5 — Validating only structure, not semantics

A Pydantic model that checks only field presence and type will accept semantically nonsensical output: a confidence score of 0.0, an effort estimate of 200 hours for a "minor typo fix", or a date in 1899. Add business-rule validators (
) to catch semantic failures that retries can also correct.

content: Copy the exact exception message or Pydantic error list into the repair prompt. Paraphrasing loses precision.
Show the model what it produced. This helps it identify the exact malformation rather than reasoning abstractly.
A one-line schema reminder ("priority must be one of: low, medium, high, critical") in the repair prompt prevents schema drift in long conversations.
Two repair attempts resolve the vast majority of transient failures. Three or more rarely add benefit and multiply cost on pathological inputs.

content.text: Log every failure with attempt number and error type.

content: Aggregate these logs weekly to find the most frequent failure patterns and address them in the base prompt.
Strip markdown fences, leading/trailing whitespace, and "Here is the JSON:" preambles before calling the parser. These are common and predictable.

content.text: Use typed models for schema definition.

content: Define the schema as a Pydantic model or Zod schema, not as a handwritten docstring. Typed models auto-generate JSON Schema and produce rich error messages.

content.text: Separate parse errors from validation errors.

content: Track them as distinct event types in observability. Parse errors indicate formatting issues; validation errors indicate semantic misunderstanding.

content.text: In pipelines where the base failure rate is low (under 5 %), parsing retries add negligible overhead. If the average failure rate is
, the expected number of LLM calls per extraction is:

attrs.latex: E[\text{calls}] = 1 + p + p^2 + \cdots + p^{k} \approx \frac{1 - p^{k+1}}{1 - p}

content.text: (two retries), this is approximately 1.052 — a 5 % overhead in average call count. The expected cost is dominated by the initial call; retries are negligible.

At scale, the main performance concern is concurrency. When running thousands of extractions in parallel with retry loops, bursts of failures can create correlated spikes of repair calls. Rate-limiting the retry path (e.g., exponential backoff with jitter when the LLM API is the bottleneck) prevents these spikes from degrading throughput. For most agentic pipelines where extractions are sequential within a single agent run, this is not a concern.

Repair prompts are longer than the original prompt by the size of the error message and the previous output — typically 200–500 extra tokens. At scale this means slightly higher per-failure token cost, but the savings from avoiding manual review or hard failures far outweigh it. Caching the system prompt with prefix caching (where supported by the API) reduces the token cost of repair calls since the schema and instruction prefix are unchanged.

Instructor is the most widely used library for structured LLM outputs. It wraps any LLM client, accepts a Pydantic model as the return type, and handles the parse-validate-retry loop internally. It supports Anthropic, OpenAI, Cohere, Gemini, and local models via Ollama.

import instructor
import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal

client = instructor.from_anthropic(anthropic.Anthropic())

class TaskAssessment(BaseModel):
    task_id: str
    priority: Literal["low", "medium", "high", "critical"]
    effort_hours: float
    confidence: float

result = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Assess: migrate auth to OAuth2, blocking enterprise launch."}],
    response_model=TaskAssessment,
    max_retries=2,  # Instructor handles the retry loop
)

print(result)  # TaskAssessment(task_id='...', priority='critical', ...)
that integrate with its chain primitives.
wraps any parser and adds one retry with error feedback — equivalent to the manual loop above but expressed declaratively.

from langchain.output_parsers import PydanticOutputParser, OutputFixingParser
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-opus-4-5")
base_parser = PydanticOutputParser(pydantic_object=TaskAssessment)
fixing_parser = OutputFixingParser.from_llm(parser=base_parser, llm=llm)

# fixing_parser.parse(raw_output) will retry once on failure

) and Anthropic (via tool use with a single tool) offer constrained-decoding modes that guarantee schema-valid output at the API level — no retry logic needed. This is the lowest-latency approach when available, but it requires the provider to support the specific schema type (no recursive schemas, limited enum support) and is not available for all models or regions.
Several approaches address the structured-output reliability problem. Each has a distinct trade-off profile.
Low on success, +1 call on failure

When validation error text is inserted into the repair prompt, it creates a potential injection surface if the error message itself contains user-supplied data. For example, if a field value that the model hallucinated contains the string "Ignore previous instructions and...", that string ends up in the repair prompt's error context. Mitigate this by sanitizing or truncating error messages before insertion, and by using structured error formatting (key: message pairs) rather than embedding raw field values.

An adversarial user who can control the input to a parsing pipeline may craft inputs that reliably cause validation failures, forcing maximum retries for every call and multiplying API costs by three. Rate-limit retry attempts per user or session, set overall cost budgets, and alert on elevated failure rates.

Parsing retries guarantee structural validity but not semantic correctness. A model may produce a schema-valid object with plausible-looking field values that are factually wrong for the given input. Downstream systems should treat parsed LLM output as unverified and apply business-rule validation (range checks, cross-field consistency checks, database lookups) before acting on it.
Set an explicit max_retries ceiling (2 is the recommended default).
Log every failed parse attempt with the full error, attempt count, and sanitized input summary.
Implement a fallback path that handles all-retries-exhausted gracefully — raise a structured exception, return a sentinel, or queue for human review.
Alert on elevated parse failure rates (e.g., > 10 % over a 1-hour window) as a sign of prompt degradation or model behavior drift.
Sanitize validation error messages before inserting into repair prompts to prevent injection.
Test the retry loop with deliberately malformed LLM responses in unit tests — do not rely on real LLM calls in CI.

Think of the LLM as a capable but occasionally careless typist. When asked to fill out a form, it sometimes skips a required field or writes a word where a number is expected. The parsing retry loop is the equivalent of a reviewer who passes the form back with specific correction notes: "The 'effort_hours' field must be a number, not the word 'medium'; the 'priority' field is missing entirely." Given that targeted feedback, the typist almost always fixes it on the first try. The reviewer only escalates to a manager (fallback) when corrections fail repeatedly — a rare event.

This mental model highlights why the error message quality matters more than the number of retries. A vague error ("invalid output") is like a reviewer saying "this is wrong" without specifying what. A precise error ("field 'confidence' must be between 0.0 and 1.0, got 1.5") gives the model a clear target. Invest in clear, structured error formatting and the retry loop performs far better than increasing max_retries.
Retry with targeted feedback, not blind regeneration.

content: Include the exact validation error and the previous output in every repair prompt so the model has a specific target to fix.

content.text: Two retries resolves the vast majority of failures.

content: Setting max_retries = 2 (three total calls) captures nearly all transient parse failures without runaway cost.

content.text: Parsing and schema validation are separate steps.

content: Catch JSON parse errors first, then validate the parsed dict against the schema. Each step has distinct error types and distinct repair strategies.
Strip markdown fences and prose preamble before calling json.loads() — these predictable patterns account for a large fraction of parse failures.

content.text: Use typed schemas (Pydantic, Zod) for rich error messages.

content: Field-level validation errors from typed models are more informative than hand-rolled checks and produce better repair prompts.

content.text: Log every failure as a KPI.

content: The per-call parse failure rate is a leading indicator of prompt quality. Track it, trend it, and use it to drive prompt improvements.

content.text: Structured output APIs eliminate retries where available.

content: When the provider offers constrained decoding (OpenAI JSON schema mode, Anthropic tool use), prefer it over retry loops for lowest latency and cost.
Always apply business-rule validation downstream of the retry loop — a schema-valid object can still be semantically wrong.

content.text: Sanitize error messages in repair prompts.

content: Validation errors that embed user-controlled content create prompt injection surfaces. Format errors as key: message pairs and truncate raw field values.
